analogy: Think of "Through the Eyes of Travellers" through the chapter's own opening images: 115 Women and men have travelled in search of work, to escape from natural disasters, as traders, merchants, soldiers, priests, pilgrims, or driven by a sense of adventure. Those who visit or come to stay in a new land invariably encounter a world that is... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THEME, FIVE, THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 116, 1.1 From Khwarizm to the Punjab. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Through the Eyes of Travellers is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 115 Women and men have travelled in search of work, to escape from natural disasters, as traders, merchants, soldiers, priests, pilgrims, or driven by a sense of adventure.

The text explains that those who visit or come to stay in a new land invariably encounter a world that is different: in terms of the landscape or physical environment as well as customs, languages, beliefs and practices of people.

The text explains that many of them try to adapt to these differences; others, somewhat exceptional, note them carefully in accounts, generally recording what they find unusual or remarkable.

The text explains that unfortunately, we have practically no accounts of travel left by women, though we know that they travelled.

The text explains that the accounts that survive are often varied in terms of their subject matter.

The text explains that some deal with affairs of the court, while others are mainly focused on religious issues, or architectural features and monuments.

The text explains that for example, one of the most important descriptions of the city of Vijayanagara (Chapter 7) in the fifteenth century comes from Abdur Razzaq Samarqandi, a diplomat who came visiting from Herat.

The text explains that in a few cases, travellers did not go to distant lands.

The text explains that for example, in the Mughal Empire (Chapters 8 and 9), administrators sometimes travelled within the empire and recorded their observations.

The text explains that some of them were interested in looking at popular customs and the folklore and traditions of their own land.

The text explains that in this chapter we shall see how our knowledge of the past can be enriched through a consideration of descriptions of social life provided by travellers who visited the subcontinent, focusing on the accounts of three men: Al-Biruni who came from Uzbekistan (eleventh century), Ibn Battuta who came from Morocco, in northwestern Africa (fourteenth century) and the Frenchman François Bernier (seventeenth century).

The text explains that through the Eyes of Travellers Perceptions of Society Perceptions of Society Perceptions of Society Perceptions of Society Perceptions of Society ((((( c c c c c. tenth to seventeenth century) . tenth to seventeenth century) . tenth to seventeenth century) . tenth to seventeenth century) . tenth to seventeenth century) THEME FIVE Fig.

The text explains that 5.1b A coconut The coconut and the paan were things that struck many travellers as unusual.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 116 As these authors came from vastly different social and cultural environments, they were often more attentive to everyday activities and practices which were taken for granted by indigenous writers, for whom these were routine matters, not worthy of being recorded.

The text explains that it is this difference in perspective that makes the accounts of travellers interesting.

The text explains that as we will see, the answers vary from one instance to the next.

The text explains that al-Biruni’s objectives Al-Biruni described his work as: a help to those who want to discuss religious questions with them (the Hindus), and as a repertory of information to those who want to associate with them.

The text explains that source 1 Ü Read the excerpt from Al-Biruni (Source 5) and discuss whether his work met these objectives.

The text explains that it is a voluminous text, divided into 80 chapters on subjects such as religion and philosophy, festivals, astronomy, alchemy, manners and customs, social life, weights and measures, iconography, laws and metrology.

The text explains that generally (though not always), Al-Biruni adopted a distinctive structure in each chapter, beginning with a question, following this up with a description based on Sanskritic traditions, and concluding with a comparison with other cultures.

The text explains that some present-day scholars have argued that this almost geometric structure, remarkable for its precision and predictability, owed much to his mathematical orientation.

The text explains that unlike most other members of his class, Ibn Battuta considered experience gained through travels to be a more important source of knowledge than books.

The text explains that he took a ship to Sumatra, and from there another ship for the Chinese port town of Source 2 Fig.

The text explains that here is an excerpt from the Rihla: I was attacked by the fever, and I actually tied myself on the saddle with a turban- cloth in case I should fall off by reason of my weakness ...

The text explains that on all sides they came forward with greetings and questions to one another, but not a soul said a word of greeting to me, since there was none of them I knew.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 120 Travelling was also more insecure: Ibn Battuta was attacked by bands of robbers several times.

The text explains that 121 Education and entertainment This is what Ibn Juzayy, who was deputed to write what Ibn Battuta dictated, said in his introduction: A gracious direction was transmitted (by the ruler) that he (Ibn Battuta) should dictate an account of the cities which he had seen in his travel, and of the interesting events which had clung to his memory, and that he should speak of those whom he had met of the rulers of countries, of their distinguished men of learning, and their pious saints.

The text explains that among the best known of the Portuguese writers is Duarte Barbosa, who wrote a detailed account of trade and society in south India.

The text explains that later, after 1750, when Indians like Shaikh Itisamuddin and Mirza Abu Talib visited Europe and confronted this image that Europeans had of their society, they tried to influence i t b y p r o d u c i n g t h e i r o w n version of matters. Ü Discuss...

The text explains that source 4 THROUGH THE EYES OF TRAVELLERS Ü What are the things from Bernier’s list that you would take on a journey today?

The text explains that what is interesting is that even though he was aware of these problems, Al-Biruni depended almost exclusively on the works of Brahmanas, often citing passages from the Vedas, the Puranas, the Bhagavad Gita, the works of Patanjali, the Manusmriti, etc., to provide an understanding of Indian society.

The text explains that 4.2 Al-Biruni’s description of the caste system Al-Biruni tried to explain the caste system by looking for parallels in other societies.

The text explains that in spite of his acceptance of the Brahmanical description of the caste system, Al-Biruni disapproved of the notion of pollution.

The text explains that 125 were not so, insisted Al-Biruni, life on earth would have been impossible.

The text explains that the conception of social pollution, intrinsic to the caste system, was according to him, contrary to the laws of nature.

The text explains that the system of varnas This is Al-Biruni’s account of the system of varnas: The highest caste are the Brahmana, of whom the books of the Hindus tell us that they were created from the head of Brahman.

The text explains that as we have seen, Al-Biruni’s description of the caste system was deeply influenced by his study of normative Sanskrit texts which laid down the rules governing the system from the point of view of the Brahmanas.

The text explains that however, in real life the system was not quite as rigid.

The text explains that for instance, the categories defined as antyaja (literally, born outside the system) were often expected to provide inexpensive labour to both peasants and zamindars (see also Chapter 8).

The text explains that source 5 Ü Compare what Al-Biruni wrote with Source 6, Chapter 3.

The text explains that do you think Al-Biruni depended only on Sanskrit texts for his information and understanding of Indian society?

The text explains that source 6 Ü What are the comparisons that Ibn Battuta makes to give his readers an idea about what coconuts looked like?

The text explains that source 7 Ü Why do you think this attracted Ibn Battuta’s attention?

The text explains that 127 5.2 Ibn Battuta and Indian cities Ibn Battuta found cities in the subcontinent full of exciting opportunities for those who had the necessary drive, resources and skills.

The text explains that it appears from Ibn Battuta’s account that most cities had crowded streets and bright and colourful markets that were stacked with a wide variety of goods.

The text explains that source 8 Dehli Here is an excerpt from Ibn Battuta’s account of Delhi, often spelt as Dehli in texts of the period: The city of Dehli covers a wide area and has a large population ...

The text explains that i have often admired the beauty, softness, and delicacy of their paintings.

The text explains that compare these with the descriptions of artisanal activity in the chapter.

The text explains that indo-Persian Travels in the Age of Discoveries, 1400-1800.

The text explains that map work If you would like to know more, read: For more information, you could visit: www.edumaritime.org THROUGH THE EYES OF TRAVELLERS 10.

The text explains that on an outline map of the world mark the countries visited by Ibn Battuta.

The text explains that interview any one of your older relatives (mother/ father/grandparents/uncles/aunts) who has travelled outside your town or village.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History II, Chapter 1: Through the Eyes of Travellers

schemes.name: He
schemes.point: He then went back once more to the Malabar coast and the Maldives, and before resuming his mission to China, visited Bengal and Assam as well. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History II Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: His
schemes.point: His idea seems to have been to influence policy-makers and the intelligentsia to ensure that they made what he considered to be the “right” decisions. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History II Ch1 pg 15)

prelims_traps: Do not treat THEME and FIVE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Through the Eyes of Travellers; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Through the Eyes of Travellers through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THEME and FIVE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Through the Eyes of Travellers, which statement is supported by the source text about 1.2 The Kitab-ul- Hind?
options: Al-Biruni and the Kitab-ul-Hind 1.1 From Khwarizm to the Punjab Al-Biruni was born in 973, in Khwarizm in present- day Uzbekistan.
Khwarizm was an important centre of learning, and Al-Biruni received the best education available at the time.
He was well versed in several languages: Syriac, Arabic, Persian, Hebrew and Sanskrit.
Although he did not know Greek, he was familiar with the works of Plato and other Greek philosophers, having read them in Arabic translations.
